title_en: Legal and Social Traditions: Hanafi Jurisprudence in Dialogue
title_ar: التقاليد الفقهية والاجتماعية: الفقه الحنفي في حوار
title_id: Tradisi-Tradisi Hukum dan Sosial: Fikih Hanafi dalam Dialog
content_id:
Bagian-bagian hukum dan sosial dari Umdat al-Qari mencakup seluruh rentang yurisprudensi Islam dari perspektif Hanafi, membahas tradisi-tradisi komersial, keluarga, pidana, dan sosial dari Sahih al-Bukhari dengan keterlibatan ketat yang sama yang mencirikan bagian-bagian ibadah. Bagian-bagian komersial sangat penting untuk memahami pendekatan Hanafi terhadap hukum komersial Islam, yang mencakup beberapa posisi khas — terutama tentang syarat-syarat transaksi ribawi dan kebolehan jenis-jenis kontrak tertentu — yang al-Ayni pertahankan dengan mengacu cermat pada bukti Bukhari.

Bagian-bagian hukum keluarga dalam syarah membahas banyak pertanyaan yang diperdebatkan tentang pernikahan, talak, dan pengasuhan dengan posisi-posisi Hanafi yang diartikulasikan dan dipertahankan dengan jelas. Pendekatan mazhab Hanafi terhadap talak — termasuk posisinya tentang ketidakbatalan talak tiga yang diucapkan dalam satu majelis dan syarat-syarat pernikahan yang sah — berbeda dari mazhab lainnya, dan syarah al-Ayni menyediakan artikulasi paling lengkap yang tersedia dari pembacaan Hanafi atas bukti kenabian tentang hal-hal ini.

Bagian-bagian hukum pidana menyajikan posisi-posisi Hanafi tentang hukuman-hukuman tetap dengan mengacu pada tradisi Bukhari yang relevan. Pendekatan khas mazhab Hanafi terhadap persyaratan pembuktian untuk hukuman pidana — termasuk beberapa posisi yang lebih ketat dari mazhab lain dan beberapa yang lebih permisif — diartikulasikan dalam bagian-bagian ini dengan mengacu pada bukti hadis yang mendasarinya.

Bagian-bagian etika sosial dari Umdat al-Qari membahas tradisi-tradisi tentang perlakuan terhadap keluarga, tetangga, dan komunitas yang lebih luas dengan kepekaan terhadap interpretasi Hanafi yang khas atas kewajiban-kewajiban ini. Pembahasan-pembahasan al-Ayni tentang bagian-bagian ini sering mencakup catatan historis tentang kondisi sosial komunitas Muslim awal yang menerangi konteks di mana tradisi-tradisi ini diterima dan diterapkan, menambahkan dimensi historis pada analisis yurisprudensial yang memperkaya kontribusi keseluruhan syarah.
content_en:
The legal and social sections of Umdat al-Qari cover the full range of Islamic jurisprudence from the Hanafi perspective, addressing the commercial, family, criminal, and social traditions of Sahih al-Bukhari with the same rigorous engagement that characterizes the worship sections. The commercial sections are particularly important for understanding the Hanafi approach to Islamic commercial law, which includes several distinctive positions — particularly on the conditions of ribawi (usurious) transactions and the permissibility of certain types of contracts — that al-Ayni defends with careful reference to the Bukhari evidence.

The family law sections of the commentary address the many contested questions of marriage, divorce, and custody with the Hanafi positions clearly articulated and defended. The Hanafi school's approach to divorce — including its positions on the irrevocability of triple divorce pronounced in a single sitting and on the conditions of valid marriage — differed from those of other schools, and al-Ayni's commentary provides the fullest available articulation of the Hanafi reading of the prophetic evidence on these matters.

The criminal law sections present the Hanafi positions on the fixed penalties with reference to the relevant Bukhari traditions. The Hanafi school's distinctive approach to the evidentiary requirements for criminal punishments — including some positions more restrictive than other schools and some more permissive — is articulated in these sections with reference to the underlying hadith evidence that supports them.

The social ethics sections of Umdat al-Qari address the traditions on the treatment of family, neighbors, and the broader community with sensitivity to the specifically Hanafi interpretation of these obligations. Al-Ayni's discussions of these sections often include historical notes on the social conditions of the early Muslim community that illuminate the context in which these traditions were received and applied, adding a historical dimension to the juristic analysis that enriches the commentary's overall contribution.
content_ar: The legal and social sections of Umdat al-Qari cover the full range of Islamic jurisprudence from the الحنفي perspective, addressing the commercial, الأسرة, criminal, and social traditions of صحيح البخاري with the same rigorous engagement that characterizes the worship sections. The commercial sections are particularly المهم for الفهم the الحنفي approach to Islamic commercial law, which includes several distinctive positions — particularly on the conditions of ribawi (usurious) transactions and the permissibility of certain types of contracts — that al-Ayni defends with careful reference to the البخاري evidence. The الأسرة law sections of the commentary address the many contested questions of marriage, divorce, and custody with the الحنفي positions clearly articulated and defended. The الحنفي school's approach to divorce — بما في ذلك its positions on the irrevocability of triple divorce pronounced in a single sitting and on the conditions of valid marriage — differed from those of other schools, and al-Ayni's commentary provides the fullest available articulation of the الحنفي reading of the prophetic evidence on these matters. The criminal law sections present the الحنفي positions on the fixed penalties with reference to the relevant البخاري traditions. The الحنفي school's distinctive approach to the evidentiary requirements for criminal punishments — بما في ذلك some positions more restrictive than other schools and some more permissive — is articulated in these sections with reference to the underlying الحديث evidence that supports them. The social ethics sections of Umdat al-Qari address the traditions on the treatment of الأسرة, neighbors, and the broader الجماعة with sensitivity to the specifically الحنفي interpretation of these obligations. Al-Ayni's discussions of these sections often include historical notes on the social conditions of the early مسلم الجماعة that illuminate the context in which these traditions were received and applied, adding a historical dimension to the juristic analysis that enriches the commentary's overall contribution.
